claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and in approved education
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child works 24+ hours/week but is in approved education
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 17 and in approved education; Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child works 24+ hours/week but is in approved education; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is 17 and in approved education
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child works 24+ hours/week but is in approved education
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! To get started, I need to ask you some questions about your child (or children).

**First, let's talk about the child's age:**

Child Benefit eligibility depends on the age of the child. Could you tell me:

1. **How old is your child?** (If you have multiple children, let's start with one at a time, and we can cover each child separately)
2. **What is your child's name?** (This helps me keep track if you have multiple children)

The basic age rules are:
- Children **under 16** are generally eligible
- Children **aged 16-19** may be eligible if they're in approved education or training
- Children **aged 20 or over** are not eligible

Please share your child's age and name so we can continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi, yes I'd like to check if I'm eligible for Child Benefit. I have one child - my son Alex. He's 17 years old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! So you have one child, Alex, who is 17 years old.

Since Alex is between 16 and 19 years old, his eligibility for Child Benefit depends on whether he's in approved education or training. Let me navigate to the next step in the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect. Now I need to understand Alex's current education or training status.

**Is Alex currently in any of the following situations?**

1. **Currently in approved education or training** (such as A-levels, Scottish Highers, NVQs, or other approved courses)

2. **Recently left education or training** (within the last 20 weeks)

3. **Neither** - not in education/training and didn't recently leave

Which of these applies to Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex is currently in education - he's studying for his A-levels.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Since Alex is currently in education studying for his A-levels, let's continue with the assessment to check if this qualifies for Child Benefit.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! A-levels are approved (non-advanced) education for Child Benefit purposes. Now I need to check a few more details about Alex's education:

**Please confirm the following:**

1. **Is Alex's A-level course full-time?** (This means at least 12 hours per week of supervised study - classroom time, not just homework)

2. **Is this course funded by an employer** or part of an arrangement where Alex has an agreed future job with an employer?

3. **Is Alex married or in a civil partnership?** (This only matters if he is - married 16-19 year olds must be financially dependent on you to qualify)

These details help me determine if the education qualifies for Child Benefit.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, it's a full-time A-level course - he's at college most weekdays. No, it's not funded by any employer, just a regular sixth form course. And no, he's not married.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! That's very helpful. Alex's A-level course meets all the requirements:
- ✓ Full-time (12+ hours per week of supervised study)
- ✓ Non-advanced education (A-levels are approved)
- ✓ Not employer-funded
- ✓ Not married

Let me continue to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! Now I need to check Alex's work situation.

**Is Alex working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work at all)?**

For Child Benefit purposes, if a 16-19 year old works 24 hours or more per week, they're not eligible - even if they're also in education. Part-time work (under 24 hours) is fine, and no work at all is also fine.

What's Alex's situation with paid work?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Actually, Alex does work quite a bit - he has a part-time job at the local supermarket and it's pretty much full-time hours alongside his studies. He works about 24 hours a week or maybe a bit more.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. This is an important detail.

Since Alex is working 24 hours per week or more, this affects his eligibility for Child Benefit. The rules state that children aged 16+ who work 24 hours or more per week are not eligible for Child Benefit, even if they're also in approved education.

Let me process this through the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Child Benefit Eligibility Assessment - Result

I'm sorry to inform you that based on the information you've provided, **you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex** at this time.

### Why Alex is not eligible:

Even though Alex is:
- ✓ 17 years old (within the 16-19 age range)
- ✓ Studying A-levels full-time (approved non-advanced education)
- ✓ Not in employer-funded training
- ✓ Not married

The issue is that **Alex is working 24 or more hours per week**. According to Child Benefit rules, children aged 16 or over who work 24+ hours per week are not eligible for Child Benefit, even if they are also in approved education.

### Important Information:

**If Alex's work situation changes:**
- If Alex reduces his work hours to **less than 24 hours per week** while continuing his A-level studies, he would become eligible again
- You can make a new claim at that point

**When the A-levels finish:**
- Child Benefit can continue through the summer after Alex finishes his A-levels (there's a 20-week extension period)
- If he goes to university or starts advanced education (like a degree), he would no longer be eligible

I understand this may be disappointing news, especially as Alex is working hard both studying and working. If his work hours do decrease, don't hesitate to reapply for Child Benefit.

Is there anything else you'd like me to clarify about this decision?

reasonings: Alex is 17 years old and studying A-levels full-time at college, which is approved non-advanced education. However, Alex is working 24 hours or more per week at a local supermarket. According to Child Benefit rules, children aged 16 or over who work 24 or more hours per week are not eligible for Child Benefit, even if they are also in approved education. Therefore, Alex does not qualify as a qualifying child for Child Benefit purposes.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex (17 years old), who is in approved education (A-levels) but is working 24+ hours per week. The work hours rule disqualifies Alex from Child Benefit eligibility despite being in approved education.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.description: For 16-19 year olds, check if in education/training or eligible for extension
response.routing_logic: If in education/training, validate type and level. If just left education (within 20 weeks), check extension eligibility.

response.question: Is the child in approved (non-advanced) education or training?
response.help_text: Approved education: A-levels, T-levels, NVQ Level 3 or below, Scottish Highers/Advanced Highers. Advanced (NOT approved): University degrees, HNC, HND, NVQ Level 4+. Must be full-time (12+ hours/week supervised study).

criteria.must_not_be_married_unless_dependent: Married 16-19 year olds only qualify if financially dependent on claimant

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.reason: Child aged 16+ is working 24 or more hours per week and not in approved education or training
response.guidance: Child Benefit stops if child aged 16 or over starts paid work for 24+ hours per week and is no longer in approved education or training. If they return to education/training, you may be able to claim again.

However, Alex is working 24 hours or more per week at a local supermarket. According to Child Benefit rules, children aged 16 or over who work 24 or more hours per week are not eligible for Child Benefit, even if they are also in approved education. Therefore, Alex does not qualify as a qualifying child for Child Benefit purposes.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex (17 years old), who is in approved education (A-levels) but is working 24+ hours per week. The work hours rule disqualifies Alex from Child Benefit eligibility despite being in approved education.
